title: Recursive functions

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Recursive functions; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A recursive C function requires a reachable base case and a call argument that progresses toward it, with each active call owning separate automatic objects.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A factorial function handles n <= 1 directly and calls factorial(n - 1) only for positive bounded input. Your goal is to write, trace and test a recursive function with explicit base and progress cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: while, do and for; pf abstraction calls; Parameters and return values; pf abstraction recursion. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A recursive C function requires a reachable base case and a call argument that progresses toward it, with each active call owning separate automatic objects. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: The C standard does not guarantee tail-call optimization, deep recursion can exhaust resources, and unsigned progress can wrap away from a base case. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Tail-recursive C code is guaranteed to use constant stack space because the recursive call appears last.

includes: Implement terminating recursion while accounting for call-stack cost.
Write, trace and test a recursive function with explicit base and progress cases.
excludes: The C standard does not guarantee tail-call optimization, deep recursion can exhaust resources, and unsigned progress can wrap away from a base case.

outcomes: Write, trace and test a recursive function with explicit base and progress cases.

examples.title: Recursive functions: defined case
examples.situation: A factorial function handles n <= 1 directly and calls factorial(n - 1) only for positive bounded input.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A factorial function handles n <= 1 directly and calls factorial(n - 1) only for positive bounded input.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: C does not guarantee tail-call optimization; prove progress and prefer iteration when required depth can exceed safe implementation limits.
examples.result: The defined result follows because C does not guarantee tail-call optimization; prove progress and prefer iteration when required depth can exceed safe implementation limits.

examples.title: Recursive functions: boundary case
examples.situation: A recursive linked-list length handles a null pointer as zero and advances to next on every recursive call.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A recursive linked-list length handles a null pointer as zero and advances to next on every recursive call.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: The C standard does not guarantee tail-call optimization, deep recursion can exhaust resources, and unsigned progress can wrap away from a base case.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Tail-recursive C code is guaranteed to use constant stack space because the recursive call appears last.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write, trace and test a recursive function with explicit base and progress cases.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Tail-recursive C code is guaranteed to use constant stack space because the recursive call appears last.
misconceptions.correction: C does not guarantee tail-call optimization; prove progress and prefer iteration when required depth can exceed safe implementation limits.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Tail-recursive C code is guaranteed to use constant stack space because the recursive call appears last.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Recursive functions: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
The C standard does not guarantee tail-call optimization, deep recursion can exhaust resources, and unsigned progress can wrap away from a base case.

recap.summary: You should now be able to write, trace and test a recursive function with explicit base and progress cases. Keep this boundary in view: The C standard does not guarantee tail-call optimization, deep recursion can exhaust resources, and unsigned progress can wrap away from a base case.
